content:
# Matthew Chapter 28 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_028_page_001.jpg, MAT_028_page_002.jpg

### The Resurrection

1. After the Sabbath, at dawn on Sunday morning, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary went to visit the tomb.

2. Suddenly there was a violent earthquake, for an angel of the Lord came down from heaven, rolled the stone away from the entrance, and sat on it.

3. His face shone like lightning, and his clothes were white as snow.

4. The guards were so terrified that they shook and became like dead men.

5. Then the angel spoke to the women. "Don't be afraid!" he said. "I know you are looking for Jesus, who was crucified.

6. He isn't here! He has been raised from the dead, just as he said would happen. Come, see where his body was lying.

7. Now go quickly and tell his disciples that he has been raised from the dead, and that he is going ahead of you to Galilee. You will see him there. Remember what I have told you."

8. The women hurried away from the tomb, frightened yet filled with great joy, and ran to find the disciples.

9. And suddenly Jesus himself met them! "Greetings!" he said. They ran to him, grasped his feet, and worshiped him.

10. Then Jesus said to them, "Don't be afraid. Go tell my brothers to leave for Galilee, and they will see me there."

### The Guards' Report

11. While the women were on their way, some of the guards went into the city and reported to the chief priests everything that had happened.

12. The chief priests called a meeting with the elders and devised a plan. They gave the soldiers a large bribe

13. and told them, "Say, 'His disciples came during the night and stole his body while we were sleeping.'

14. If the governor hears about it, we'll stand up for you and make sure you don't get in trouble."

15. So the guards accepted the bribe and said what they were told to say. And this story has been widely circulated among the Jewish people to this day.

### The Great Commission

16. Then the eleven disciples left for Galilee, going to the mountain where Jesus had told them to go.

17. When they saw him, they worshiped him — though some of them still had doubts.

18. Jesus came to them and said, "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.

19. Therefore, go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit,

20. teaching them to obey everything I have commanded you. And remember this: I am with you always, to the very end of the age."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — The women go first:** The first witnesses to the resurrection are women — whose testimony was not considered legally valid in first-century Jewish courts. If the early church had invented the resurrection story, they would never have made women the primary witnesses. The fact that they did is one of the strongest historical arguments for the authenticity of the tradition.

**Verse 6 — "He has been raised":** Not "he rose" but "he was raised" — the passive voice points to God as the agent. The resurrection is not something Jesus did for himself but something the Father did for the Son. This is the vindication of everything Jesus claimed.

**Verse 9 — "Greetings!":** The Greek χαίρετε literally means "Rejoice!" — the most ordinary greeting in the ancient world, now spoken in the most extraordinary circumstances. The risen Jesus' first word to his followers is an everyday hello. The resurrection doesn't obliterate the ordinary — it transforms it.

**Verse 17 — "though some still had doubts":** This is perhaps the most honest sentence in the entire Gospel. At the climactic moment — the mountain in Galilee where everything comes together — some of the eleven still doubt. And Jesus gives the Great Commission anyway. He doesn't wait for perfect faith. He commissions imperfect people. The church is built not on certainty but on obedience in the midst of questions.

**Verses 18-20 — The Great Commission:** Matthew's Gospel begins with "Emmanuel — God with us" (1:23) and ends with "I am with you always, to the very end of the age." The entire Gospel is framed by divine presence. The mission is not "go and be alone" but "go, and I go with you." The scope is universal: "all authority," "all nations," "all things I have commanded," "all the days." Four uses of "all" — the mission is total.

**The trinitarian formula (v.19):** "In the name [singular] of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit." The singular "name" governing three persons is one of the earliest and most compressed statements of what would later be called the doctrine of the Trinity. One name, three persons.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
